claim.statement: Learning traps—stable false beliefs that persist despite extensive experience—can arise from selective attention interacting with choice-contingent feedback, causing learners to adopt simpler (one-dimensional) strategies and resist corrective learning even when further information is available.
claim.statement_location: Abstract / Introduction (summary of paper: description of 'learning traps' and their origin in selective attention and contingent feedback).
claim.study_type: Experimental (behavioral experiments) combined with computational simulations / modeling

data.source: Behavioral experiment data collected from human participants via Amazon Mechanical Turk (job-application style tasks) and simulation data generated from the ALCOVE-RL computational model.
data.wave_or_subset: Experiment 1 (initial study; 101 participants recruited, 95 analyzed after exclusions); Experiment 2 (intervention study; ~400 participants on MTurk); multiple simulation conditions (1,000 runs per condition reported for some simulations).
data.sample_size: Experiment 1: 101 recruited, 95 analyzed; Experiment 2: 400 participants (approximately). Simulation runs: 1,000 simulations per condition reported for main simulation analyses.
data.unit_of_analysis: Individual human participants (behavioral experiments) and individual simulation agents (model runs).
data.notes: Simulation parameter values reported (e.g., specificity constant c = 6, temperature τ = 15, learning rates η_w = 0.1 and η_a = 0.1). Feedback conditions include contingent, full-feedback, random-feedback, and constructivist coding. Data collection used MTurk; exact data repository or sharing links not stated.

method.description: The study combined computational simulations (a modified ALCOVE model called ALCOVE-RL) with behavioral experiments. Simulations tested multiple feedback conditions (contingent vs full feedback, with and without attentional learning, and random feedback) and parameterized attention and weight learning. Human experiments presented multi-dimensional decision tasks (job-application analogues) where participants received either contingent information (feedback only for chosen items) or full information (feedback for all items). Strategy adoption was measured via 1D and 2D strategy scores. Experiment 2 tested interventions (individuation, occlusion of dimensions, added noise) to assess their effect on learning traps. Bayesian hierarchical models (implemented in Stan) were used to analyze condition means and proportions.
method.steps:
1. Implement ALCOVE-RL model and set parameters (e.g., c = 6, τ = 15, η_w = 0.1, η_a = 0.1).
2. Run simulations across specified conditions (contingent-feedback with attention, full-feedback with attention, contingent without attention, full without attention, random feedback), with many runs per condition (e.g., 1,000).
3. Design behavioral task analogous to simulation (multi-feature prospects, reward structure where two features are relevant).
4. Recruit participants via Amazon Mechanical Turk.
5. Randomly assign participants to feedback conditions (contingent-information vs full-information) and to intervention conditions in Experiment 2 (individuation, occlusion, noise, control conditions).
6. Collect trial-by-trial choices, feedback responses, and compute strategy-alignment scores (1D and 2D scores) and other behavioral metrics (approach/accept rates, estimates of unsuitable applicants).
7. Analyze data using Bayesian hierarchical models (continuous outcomes: population-level normal model with Normal(0.5,1) prior for means and truncated Normal(0,0.5) for SD; binary outcomes: beta-binomial reparameterizations with Beta(2,2) and Gamma(1,0.1) priors), using Stan with multiple chains and large sample draws.
method.models: ALCOVE-RL (modified ALCOVE for reinforcement learning / attention-based category learning); Bayesian hierarchical models for condition-level inference (Stan). Statistical modeling included normal models for continuous outcomes and beta/binomial models for proportions.
method.outcome_variable: Primary outcomes: 1D strategy score (one-dimensional strategy alignment), 2D strategy score (correct two-dimensional strategy alignment), approach/acceptance rates (proportion of trials or applicants approached/accepted), estimates of unsuitable applicants (participant estimates).
method.independent_variables: Feedback condition (contingent-information vs full-information), presence of attentional learning in simulations, intervention conditions (individuation, occlusion/masking of dimensions, added noise), feedback type in simulations (random, full, contingent, constructivist coding).

results.summary: Simulations and behavioral experiments demonstrated attentional learning traps emerge when feedback is choice-contingent and learners employ selective attention. In simulations, over 25% of agents in the contingent-feedback-with-attention condition fell into learning traps (failing to integrate both relevant dimensions). Full-feedback conditions enabled rapid and accurate learning. In human experiments, contingent-information participants were more likely to adopt one-dimensional (trap) strategies and accept more applicants than full-information participants; full-information participants were more likely to adopt the correct two-dimensional strategies. Interventions (individuation, occlusion, noise) reduced learning traps in some simulations but did not robustly prevent traps in the human intervention experiment; interventions often slowed early learning and sometimes reduced long-term performance. Bayesian analyses were used to estimate condition effects and proportions.

numerical_results.outcome_name: Proportion of simulation agents falling into learning traps (contingent-feedback with attention)
numerical_results.direction: positive (meaning substantial occurrence of traps under that condition)

numerical_results.outcome_name: 1D strategy score (test phase) — contingent vs full
numerical_results.direction: contingent > full (greater one-dimensional strategy use under contingent feedback)

numerical_results.outcome_name: 2D strategy score (test phase) — contingent vs full
numerical_results.direction: contingent < full (less two-dimensional strategy adoption under contingent feedback)

numerical_results.outcome_name: Acceptance / approach rates — contingent vs full (job application task)
numerical_results.direction: contingent > full (higher acceptance in contingent-information condition)

numerical_results.outcome_name: Estimated proportion of unsuitable applicants — contingent vs full (vs actual)
